content: In Jude 1:1, how does the author identify himself?
explanation: Jude 1:1 — 'Jude, a servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, to those who are called, beloved in God the Father and kept for Jesus Christ.' Jude emphasizes his relation to Christ and to James (his brother) rather than his own renown.
options: A servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James

content: How does Jude describe the recipients of his letter?
explanation: Jude 1:1 — '...to those who are called, beloved in God the Father and kept for Jesus Christ.' Three marks: called, beloved, kept.
options: Those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ

content: According to Jude 1:3, what does the author urge believers to do?
explanation: Jude 1:3 — '...I found it necessary to write appealing to you to contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.' 'Contend' (epagōnizesthai) denotes earnest struggle to guard the faith from distortion.
options: Be silent when false teachers appear
Contend for the faith once for all delivered to the saints
Form a separate faction of believers

content: According to Jude 1:4, what have those who crept into the church done with God's grace?
explanation: Jude 1:4 — '...ungodly people, who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.'
options: Preserved and passed it on to future generations
Harmonized it with the Mosaic law
Used it to establish charitable funds
Perverted it into sensuality and denied the Master Jesus Christ

content: According to Jude 1:5, what did the Lord do to those who did not believe after saving them out of Egypt?
explanation: Jude 1:5 — '...Jesus, who saved a people out of the land of Egypt, afterward destroyed those who did not believe.' A historical example — being delivered does not secure safety if one ceases to believe.
options: Destroyed those who did not believe
Brought them immediately into the Promised Land

content: According to Jude 1:6, what happened to the angels who did not stay within their own position?
explanation: Jude 1:6 — '...the angels who did not stay within their own position of authority, but left their proper dwelling, he has kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness until the judgment of the great day.'
options: Exiled to earth to serve humanity
Kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness until the great day of judgment

content: According to Jude 1:7, what two cities serve as the third example of judgment?
explanation: Jude 1:7 — '...just as Sodom and Gomorrah and the surrounding cities, which likewise indulged in sexual immorality and pursued unnatural desire, serve as an example by undergoing a punishment of eternal fire.'

content: According to Jude 1:9, who contended with the devil about the body of Moses?
explanation: Jude 1:9 — 'But when the archangel Michael, contending with the devil, was disputing about the body of Moses, he did not presume to pronounce a blasphemous judgment, but said, "The Lord rebuke you."' This rare detail highlights the humility of a high-ranking angel.

content: What did Michael say when contending with the devil?
explanation: Jude 1:9 — '...he did not presume to pronounce a blasphemous judgment, but said, "The Lord rebuke you."' Even a high archangel did not presume to revile — a lesson in humility for those who 'blaspheme the glorious ones' (1:8).
options: You will be cast into eternal fire

content: According to Jude 1:11, the false teachers follow the way of which three Old Testament figures?
explanation: Jude 1:11 — 'Woe to them! For they walked in the way of Cain and abandoned themselves for the sake of gain to Balaam's error and perished in Korah's rebellion.'

content: According to Jude 1:14, the prophecy about judgment is quoted from whom?
explanation: Jude 1:14 — 'It was also about these that Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied, saying, "Behold, the Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones."' Enoch — 'the seventh from Adam' — is the link emphasizing the antiquity of the prophecy.
options: Melchizedek, priest of God Most High

content: According to Enoch's prophecy in Jude 1:14, with whom will the Lord come?
explanation: Jude 1:14 — '...Behold, the Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones.' 'Ten thousands of holy ones' likely refers to the holy angels present in the day of judgment.
options: Ten thousands of His holy ones

content: How does Jude 1:19 describe the false teachers?
explanation: Jude 1:19 — 'It is these who cause divisions, worldly people, devoid of the Spirit.' Three marks: divisive, worldly, without the Spirit.
options: They cause divisions, are worldly, and are devoid of the Spirit
Full of Scripture knowledge but lacking compassion
Ascetic and given to much prayer

content: According to Jude 1:20, what must believers do to build themselves up?
explanation: Jude 1:20 — 'But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith and praying in the Holy Spirit.'
options: Study the learning of the age
Build themselves up on their most holy faith and pray in the Holy Spirit

content: According to Jude 1:21, in what should believers keep themselves?
explanation: Jude 1:21 — '...keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that leads to eternal life.'
options: In the traditions of the fathers
In the love of God, waiting for the mercy of Christ unto eternal life

content: According to Jude 1:24, who is able to keep believers from stumbling?
explanation: Jude 1:24 — 'Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy...'
options: God — Him who is able to keep you from stumbling

content: What four attributes are ascribed to God in the closing doxology (Jude 1:25)?
explanation: Jude 1:25 — '...to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen.'

content: According to Jude 1:11, which three Old Testament figures are listed? (select all that apply)
explanation: Jude 1:11 — '...they walked in the way of Cain and abandoned themselves for the sake of gain to Balaam's error and perished in Korah's rebellion.' Three figures; Esau is not mentioned here.

content: Which images does Jude 1:12-13 use to describe false teachers? (select all that apply)
explanation: Jude 1:12-13 — 'These are hidden reefs at your love feasts... waterless clouds, swept along by winds; fruitless trees in late autumn, twice dead, uprooted; wild waves of the sea... wandering stars, for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved forever.' 'Lions of the field' is not used in this passage.
options: Waterless clouds swept along by winds
Wandering stars for whom utter darkness is reserved forever
Lions of the field stalking the flock

content: According to Jude 1:22-23, how should believers treat three kinds of people? (select all that apply)
explanation: Jude 1:22-23 — 'And have mercy on those who doubt; save others by snatching them out of the fire; to others show mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.' Three groups: doubters (mercy), those to be snatched from fire (save), and those needing mercy with fear.
options: Those who doubt — have mercy on them
Those to be snatched out of the fire
Those needing mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh
